items.prompt: Which property most directly justifies binary search?

options.text: After evaluating a midpoint, one side can be proven unable to contain the answer.
The input contains many elements.
The values are numeric.
The implementation uses a variable named mid.

feedback.reason: Binary search is legal only when midpoint information proves that an entire region can be discarded.
feedback.details: The decisive signal is a correctness-preserving partition of the search space, not a keyword, data type, or input size.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.large_input: Large input creates a performance need, not a correctness rule.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.numeric_values: Binary search can work on non-numeric ordered domains, and numbers alone do not create a discard rule.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_variable: A midpoint variable does not prove correctness or logarithmic progress.

items.prompt: A sorted array is searched for a value using an arbitrary predicate that can be true, then false, then true again. Is binary search justified by sorted input alone?

options.text: No. The predicate does not define one discardable boundary.
Yes. Sorted values always make every predicate binary-searchable.
Yes, as long as a midpoint is inspected.
Yes, when the array is large enough.

feedback.reason: Sorted input is not sufficient when the searched property lacks monotonic structure.
feedback.details: Binary search requires a relation between midpoint evidence and the region that can be removed. A predicate with multiple truth transitions provides no such global boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_sorted: Sorting the values does not make an unrelated predicate monotonic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_mid: Midpoint inspection needs a valid elimination rule.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_large: Size does not repair a correctness defect.

items.prompt: Which task has a clear binary-search signal?

options.text: Find the first integer x for which a monotonic predicate feasible(x) becomes true.
Count the frequency of each character in a string.
Generate every permutation of n values.
Compute all prefix sums.

feedback.reason: A monotonic false-to-true transition creates a searchable boundary.
feedback.details: Evaluating feasible(mid) identifies which side cannot contain the first true value.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.count_letters: Frequency accumulation does not halve a search domain.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_permutations: Enumeration requires exploring many branches rather than discarding one half.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.running_sum: Prefix accumulation is deterministic linear processing.

items.prompt: An unsorted array must be searched for one exact value, and no preprocessing is allowed. Which conclusion is correct?

options.text: Binary search is not justified because comparisons at mid do not eliminate either side.
Binary search is valid because exact equality can be checked at mid.
The algorithm may behave as though the input were sorted.
The parity of mid determines which side to discard.

feedback.reason: Without ordering or another monotonic structure, midpoint comparison does not provide directional evidence.
feedback.details: A linear scan remains correct because every position may contain the target.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_anyway: A mismatch at mid gives no direction in an unsorted array.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_implicitly: Correctness cannot assume an unstated order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.use_mid_parity: Index parity is unrelated to target location.

items.prompt: A problem asks for the smallest capacity that can process all jobs within a deadline, and larger capacities never make the schedule worse. What is the binary-search signal?

options.text: Feasibility changes monotonically from false to true as capacity increases.
The jobs can be sorted.
Capacity is numeric.
The deadline value is large.

feedback.reason: The answer domain has a first feasible capacity.
feedback.details: Testing a candidate capacity proves that all smaller or all larger candidates can be discarded, depending on the result.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.jobs_sorted: Sorting may help the predicate but does not define the answer boundary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.capacity_numeric: A numeric domain alone is not enough.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.deadline_large: Magnitude does not establish a discard rule.

items.prompt: Which statement is an unsafe heuristic?

options.text: "The input is sorted, so binary search must be the best choice."
"A midpoint comparison proves one region cannot contain the answer."
"The predicate has one truth transition over an ordered domain."
"In a rotated array with distinct values, one half is still ordered."

feedback.reason: Sorted input is helpful only when the query and cost model make midpoint elimination legal and useful.
feedback.details: A one-off linear scan may be simpler, and some predicates over sorted data remain non-monotonic.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.discard_rule: This is the central correctness argument.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_half: This can support a rotated-array discard rule.

items.prompt: Why does using left, right, and mid not by itself prove O(log n) time?

options.text: The active search interval must shrink by a constant factor and midpoint work must be bounded.
Variable names determine asymptotic complexity.
Any algorithm with three variables is logarithmic.
Integer indices always produce halving.

feedback.reason: Logarithmic iteration count comes from strict halving of the remaining domain.
feedback.details: If updates retain the same interval or the predicate evaluation is expensive, the total complexity can be non-logarithmic.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.three_variables: Variable count does not determine iteration count.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.integer_indices: Updates may fail to shrink or may move by one position.

items.prompt: A rotated sorted array contains distinct values. Why can binary search still be legal?

options.text: At least one half of the active interval is ordered, allowing a target-range test.
Rotation automatically disappears after choosing mid.
All values become equal after rotation.
The pivot is always given explicitly.

feedback.reason: Distinct-value rotation preserves enough local order to identify one sorted half.
feedback.details: The target's membership in that ordered half determines which region can be discarded.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.pivot_known: Rotated search can work without a known pivot.

items.prompt: A predicate over indices follows false, true, false, true. What is the correct diagnosis?

options.text: It is not a valid single-boundary predicate for ordinary binary search.
It is valid because a first true value exists.
It is valid because indices are ordered.
Choosing a different midpoint formula makes it monotonic.

feedback.reason: Ordinary boundary search needs one directional truth transition.
feedback.details: Multiple transitions mean a midpoint result does not eliminate an entire side safely.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_true_valid: Existence of a first true does not imply later values remain true.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_indices: An ordered domain is necessary but not sufficient.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_fixes: Midpoint bias cannot change predicate semantics.

items.prompt: A sorted array is queried once for whether any element satisfies an expensive non-monotonic condition. What should determine the strategy?

options.text: Whether the condition supports a legal elimination rule, not merely that the values are sorted.
Binary search is always faster on sorted arrays.
Sort the array again before searching.
Check only the midpoint and return its condition result.

feedback.reason: The query semantics determine whether sorted order is useful.
feedback.details: Binary search is a reasoning pattern built on elimination, not a general-purpose shortcut for sorted inputs.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_binary: No correctness rule has been established.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_sort_again: The array is already sorted, and sorting does not make the predicate monotonic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_sample: One sample cannot answer an existential query without structure.

items.prompt: Which problem most naturally supports searching for the last valid value?

options.text: Find the greatest threshold for which a monotonic feasibility predicate remains true.
Find any local maximum in an arbitrary unsorted sequence using the same rule for every input.
Count occurrences in an unsorted array without preprocessing.
Generate every subset.

feedback.reason: A true-to-false predicate over an ordered domain defines a last-true boundary.
feedback.details: Midpoint feasibility determines which side can still contain a larger valid threshold.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_local_max: No monotonic predicate has been established.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.enumerate_subsets: Enumeration does not discard one region.

items.prompt: When can repeated queries make preprocessing for binary search attractive?

options.text: When one preprocessing cost enables many later logarithmic queries and the order contract permits it.
For every single query regardless of preprocessing cost.
When original order must be preserved but indices are discarded.
When the query predicate remains non-monotonic after preprocessing.

feedback.reason: Strategy choice should include both preprocessing and query volume.
feedback.details: A sorted or indexed representation can pay off across many searches, but only if it preserves the required semantics.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_query_always: A one-off sort can cost more than a scan.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.order_must_preserve: Destroying required order may violate the contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.predicate_nonmonotonic: Binary search still lacks a valid boundary.

items.prompt: Which features are strong binary-search signals? Select all that apply.

options.text: The candidate domain has a total order.
Midpoint evidence proves an entire region cannot contain the answer.
A predicate changes truth value at most once in the searched direction.
The input is large, with no other structure.

feedback.reason: Binary search needs an ordered domain, a single-boundary structure, and a legal discard rule.
feedback.details: Performance pressure may motivate the search for such structure but does not create it.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.large_only: Size alone gives no correctness proof.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.single_transition: A single transition defines a boundary.

items.prompt: Which observations are insufficient by themselves to justify binary search? Select all that apply.

options.text: The data values are sorted.
The proposed code uses a midpoint variable.
The desired answer is numeric.
The predicate is proven monotonic over an ordered finite domain.

feedback.reason: Surface properties do not replace the half-discard proof.
feedback.details: A proven monotonic predicate is different because it directly establishes the searchable boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.monotonic_predicate: This is a substantive binary-search foundation.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_values: The query may not align with that order.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.uses_mid: Syntax does not prove elimination or progress.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.numeric_answer: Numeric domains may still have non-monotonic feasibility.

items.prompt: Which tasks can legally use a binary-search family mechanism? Select all that apply.

options.text: Find an exact target in ascending sorted data.
Find the first true value of a false-to-true monotonic predicate.
Find a target in a rotated sorted array with distinct values.
Find a target in arbitrary unsorted data without preprocessing.

feedback.reason: Each accepted task has a specific invariant that supports directional elimination.
feedback.details: The unsorted exact-search case does not.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_unsorted: A midpoint mismatch gives no direction.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exact_sorted: Comparison determines the only side where the target can remain.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.first_true: The truth transition is a searchable boundary.

items.prompt: A binary search examines O(log W) candidate values, and evaluating the monotonic predicate once costs P(n). Select the total time and iterative auxiliary space.
constraints: W is the size of the ordered candidate range.
The predicate does not allocate growing persistent state between iterations.

feedback.reason: Binary search controls the number of predicate calls, not the internal cost of one call.
feedback.details: The total time multiplies predicate cost by logarithmic iterations. An iterative search needs only boundary variables outside predicate-local memory.
